1: ḥasdê YHWH lĕʿôlām ʾāshîrāh — I will sing of the LORD's steadfast love forever. The opening declaration sets ḥesed (steadfast love, covenant loyalty) as the psalm's governing word — it appears more than a dozen times across the 52 verses. lĕdōr wādōr ʾôdîaʿ ʾĕmûnātĕkhā bĕphî — with my mouth I will proclaim your faithfulness to every generation. The double temporal scope (forever + every generation) frames what becomes in vv38-51 an agonized question: where is the ḥesed that was proclaimed?
2: kî-ʾāmartî ʿôlām ḥesed yibbāneh — for I declared, your steadfast love is built to stand forever. Yibbāneh (niphal of bānāh: to be built, to be established — the passive form: it is being built, is under construction that will never stop) — ḥesed as an edifice God is perpetually constructing. shāmayim tākhîn ʾĕmûnātĕkhā bāhem — your faithfulness is established in the heavens themselves. Tākhîn (hiphil of kûn: to establish firmly) — the divine faithfulness as permanent as the heavens: what the heavens are to the physical cosmos, faithfulness is to the moral cosmos.
3: kārattî berît libbĕḥîrî — I have made a covenant with my chosen one. The divine speech begins (vv3-4) — God quotes himself in the context of the Davidic covenant. Bāḥar (to choose — the election verb of Deuteronomy; David chosen as YHWH chose Israel). nishbaʿtî lĕdāwid ʿabdî — I have sworn an oath to David my servant. The covenant is secured by divine oath — not merely promise but sworn commitment, which makes it the most binding form of divine communication (cf. Ps 89:35; Heb 6:17-18).
4: ʿad-ʿôlām ʾăkhîn zarʿekā — I will make your line endure forever. Zāraʿ (seed, offspring, line — the Abrahamic promise vocabulary of Gen 12:7; 13:15) applied to David: the Davidic covenant inherits the Abrahamic seed-promise. ûbānîtî lĕdōr-wādōr kissĕʾăkhā selāh — and build your throne for every generation to come. Bānāh (to build) — the same verb as v2's ḥesed yibbāneh: the steadfast love is built by building the throne. The selah marks the weight of the oath just recited.
5: wĕyôdû shāmayim pilʾĕkhā YHWH — the heavens praise your wonders, O LORD. From covenant declaration (vv3-4) to cosmic worship (vv5-7). Pilʾĕkhā (your wonders — the Exodus word, now applied to heavenly wonder-works). ʾap-ʾĕmûnātĕkhā biqhal qĕdōshîm — your faithfulness is declared in the congregation of the holy ones. Qĕhal qĕdōshîm (the congregation/assembly of the holy ones — the heavenly council of divine beings) — the faithfulness proclaimed on earth (v1-2) is simultaneously declared in the heavenly realm.
6: kî mî bashshaḥaq yaʿărōkh laYHWH — for who in all the heavens can be compared with the LORD? Shaḥaq (the thin upper air, the clouds — the highest stratum of the visible sky, where the divine beings dwell). yidmeh laYHWH bibnê ʾēlîm — who among the heavenly beings is like the LORD? Bĕnê ʾēlîm (sons of God/divine beings — the heavenly council members, cf. Ps 29:1; 82:1). The incomparability formula of v8 (= Ps 86:8) applied to the heavenly council: YHWH is incomparable even among divine beings.
7: ʾêl naʿărāṣ bĕsôd-qĕdōshîm rabbāh — a God greatly feared in the assembly of the holy ones. Naʿărāṣ (niphal of yārēʾ: held in awe, feared — the passive: one before whom awe is experienced). wĕnôrāʾ ʿal-kol-sĕbîbāyw — and awesome above all who surround him. The council members who surround YHWH (as in Isa 6's seraphim, or the council of 1 Kgs 22:19-23) experience awe in his presence — divine beings awed by the one they serve.
8: YHWH ʾĕlōhê ṣĕbāʾôt mî-khāmôkhā ḥăsîn YAH — LORD God of hosts, who is as powerful as you, LORD? ʾĔlōhê ṣĕbāʾôt (God of hosts — the military title of divine sovereignty over all heavenly and earthly armies). Ḥāsîn (mighty, strong — hapax in this form; the superlative of divine power). wĕʾĕmûnātĕkhā sĕbîbôtekhā — your faithfulness wraps around you on every side. Divine faithfulness not merely as an attribute but as a surrounding environment — ʾĕmûnāh (faithfulness/reliability) as the atmosphere that surrounds the divine throne.
9: ʾattāh môshēl bĕgeʾût hayyām — you govern the surging sea. Gēʾûth (surging, swelling — the proud rising of waves, from gāʾāh: to be exalted, to surge) — the sea in its most threatening aspect. bĕśôʾ gālāyw ʾattāh tĕshabbĕḥēm — when its waves rear up, you calm them. Shābah (piel: to calm, still — used of Jesus's stilling of the storm in Mark 4:39, perhaps deliberately). YHWH's mastery over the chaos-sea continues the creation-battle theology of Ps 74:13-14.
10: ʾattāh dikkîtāh kĕḥālāl rāhab — you crushed Rahab like a slaughtered carcass. Rāhab (the chaos dragon — here as a name for Egypt, as in Ps 87:4; Isa 30:7; 51:9) crushed (dākhāh: to crush, break down) like a ḥālāl (one slain, a corpse). bizrôaʿ ʿuzzĕkhā pizzartā ʾôyĕbêkhā — with your mighty arm you scattered your enemies. The divine arm (zĕrôaʿ) — the Exodus arm, now pictured scattering the chaos-forces as it scattered Pharaoh's army.
11: lĕkhā shāmayim ʾaf-lĕkhā ʾāreṣ — the heavens belong to you; the earth also is yours. The creation claim: heavens and earth are YHWH's by right of creation, not by conquest. tēbēl ûmĕlōʾāh ʾattāh yĕsadtām — you founded the world and everything in it. Tēbēl (the inhabited world) + mĕlōʾāh (its fullness — everything filling it). The double claim grounds every subsequent assertion of YHWH's sovereignty.
12: ṣāphôn wĕyāmîn ʾattāh bĕrāʾtām — north and south — you created them. All four compass directions implied by the pairing of north (ṣāphôn) and south (yāmîn: literally, the right hand — south). tābôr wĕḥermôn bĕshimĕkhā yĕrannēnû — Tabor and Hermon sing for joy in your name. The two great mountains — Tabor in the west (the hill of Jezreel valley) and Hermon in the far northeast — as personified worshipers, representing the entire land in its geographic extent singing to its Creator.
13: lĕkhā zĕrôaʿ ʿim-gĕbûrāh — your arm is mighty. The divine arm (zĕrôaʿ) returns — consistently the emblem of Exodus power. tāʿōz yādĕkhā tārûm yĕmînekhā — your hand is strong; your right hand is raised high. The three body-parts of divine power: arm, hand, right hand. All three in elevated, active position — the warrior's ready posture.
14: ṣedeq ûmishpāṭ mĕkhôn kissĕʾekā — righteousness and justice are the foundation of your throne. Mĕkhôn (foundation, established base — from kûn: to be fixed, established) — the divine throne rests on ṣedeq ûmishpāṭ (righteousness and justice): these are not policies God implements but the structural support of his rule. ḥesed wĕʾemet yĕqaddĕmû phānêkhā — steadfast love and faithfulness lead the way before you. Qāddam pānim (to go before the face) — ḥesed and ʾemet as heralds preceding the divine king. Four divine attributes together form the cosmic government architecture.
15: ʾashrê hāʿām yôdĕʿê tĕrûʿāh — blessed is the people who know the joyful shout. Tĕrûʿāh (the battle cry, the festive shout, the sound of the shophar — used of the war cry in Josh 6:5,20, of the worship-shout in Ezra 3:12-13, and of the shophar blast of the new moon festival) — those who know YHWH's war-shout/worship-cry are blessed. YHWH bĕʾôr-pānêkhā yĕhallēkhûn — they walk, LORD, in the light of your face. ʾôr pānîm (the light of the face — the Aaronic blessing's yāʾēr YHWH pānāyw ʾēlêkhā, Num 6:25) — the people who know the divine shout walk in the light of divine favor.
16: bĕshimĕkhā yĕgîlûn kol-hayyôm — they rejoice in your name all day long. Gîl (to rejoice — the active, physical joy of dancing; more bodily than śāmaḥ) in the divine name all day long. ûbĕṣidqātĕkhā yārûmû — and are lifted up by your righteousness. The divine righteousness (ṣĕdāqāh) as the lifting force — the blessed people are elevated not by their own merit but by God's righteous intervention.
17: kî-tiphʾeret ʿuzzāmô ʾāttāh — for you are the splendor of their strength. Tiphʾeret (splendor, glory, beauty) — the beauty that makes their strength beautiful comes from God. ûbirṣônĕkhā tārûm qarnênû — by your favor our strength is raised high. Rāṣôn (favor, goodwill — the divine favor as the agent of horn-exaltation; cf. Ps 75's horn imagery). The community's strength is raised by divine favor, not by military capability.
18: kî laYHWH māginnênû — for our shield belongs to the LORD. Māgēn (shield — the small hand-shield, the personal protection) — Israel's protective shield is YHWH's property, not their own. wĕliqĕdôsh yisrāʾêl malkênû — our king is the Holy One of Israel. Qĕdôsh yisrāʾêl (Holy One of Israel — the Isaianic epithet, as in Pss 71:22; 78:41) — the king of Israel is not a human sovereign but the Holy One. This sets up the Davidic oracle: the human king of vv19-37 is the vice-regent of this divine king.
19: ʾāz dibbartā bĕḥāzôn laḥăsîdêkhā — you spoke in a vision to your faithful servants. Ḥāzôn (vision — the prophetic vision in which God's word comes; cf. 2 Sam 7's Nathan oracle which is the background for this Psalm). wayyōʾmer shiwwîtî ʿēzer ʿal-gibbôr — and said, I have given help to a warrior. Gîbbôr (warrior, mighty man) — David the fighting man. The divine help precedes the human achievement; military prowess is sustained from above.
20: māṣāʾtî dāwid ʿabdî — I have found David my servant. The election formula māṣāʾ (to find — not the result of a search but of an encounter: God found David as one finds an unexpected treasure) + ʿabdî (my servant — the highest honor title in the OT, borne by Moses, Abraham, the Prophets, and here David). bĕshemen qodshî māshaḥtîhû — with my holy oil I have anointed him. The anointing with shemen qōdesh (holy oil — the sacred anointing oil of Exod 30:22-32) — the physical act that transferred divine Spirit and commission.
21: ʾăsher yādî tikkôn ʿimmô — my hand will uphold him steadily. Kûn (hiphil: to make firm, establish steadily) — the divine hand as a steadying support, not merely an occasional intervention. ʾap-zĕrōʿî tĕʾammĕṣennû — my arm will give him strength. The divine arm (zĕrôaʿ) channeling strength into the king — David's military power is the Exodus arm directed through a human king.
22: lōʾ-yashîʾ ʿālaw ʾôyēb — no enemy will overpower him. Naśāʾ (hiphil: to exact tribute from, to oppress, to overpower) — no enemy will be able to extract what they want from him. ûben-ʿawlāh lōʾ yĕʿannennû — no wicked person will oppress him. The double negative of vv22-23 establishes the invulnerability of the anointed: both external enemies and internal troublemakers are blocked from oppressing the one God upholds.
23: wĕkhattôtî miqqĕdāmāyw ṣārāyw — I will shatter his enemies before his eyes. The hiphil of kāthat (to crush, pound — the mortar-and-pestle violence of crushing) — God acts as the warrior whose crushing power precedes the king into battle. ûmĕśanʾāyw ʾĕgōp — and defeat all who hate him. Nāgaph (hiphil: to strike, beat, defeat in battle — the plague verb of Exod 12:27 now applied to David's enemies).
24: wĕʾĕmûnātî wĕḥasdî ʿimmô — my faithfulness and steadfast love will be with him. The two covenant attributes (ʾĕmûnāh and ḥesed) travel with David as divine companions — they are not merely qualities God has but presences God sends with his anointed. ûbishmî tārûm qarnô — through my name his strength will be raised high. Horn-exaltation (cf. Ps 75:10; 89:17) through the divine name — the king's power is named, attributed, and sustained by YHWH's own reputation.
25: wĕśamtî bayyām yādô — I will extend his dominion to the sea. Yādô (his hand — as a symbol of rule and sovereignty) placed in the sea: the king's authority reaching to the horizon. ûbannĕhārôt yĕmînô — and his right hand over the rivers. The pairing of sea and rivers as the boundaries of universal dominion echoes Ps 72:8's Messianic scope. What Ps 72 prays for the ideal king, Ps 89 establishes as divine covenant promise.
26: hûʾ yiqrāʾēnî ʾābî ʾāttāh — he will call out to me, 'You are my Father'. ʾĀbî ʾāttāh (my Father, you) — the most intimate address. The Davidic king adopts the filial relationship with YHWH. This royal father-son language is the background for the NT's Christological use of divine sonship: what is true of the Davidic king in a representative sense is true of Jesus in its full ontological sense. ʾēlî wĕṣûr yĕshûʿātî — my God, and the Rock who saves me. The two titles from the Psalter's own vocabulary of trust (God + Rock of salvation) placed in the mouth of the anointed king.
27: ʾap-ʾănî bĕkhôr ʾettĕnēhû — I will appoint him as my firstborn. Bĕkhôr (firstborn — the one with the double-portion inheritance, the primary heir, the one who stands first before God). The Davidic king is to have the status of firstborn before YHWH — which Israel as a whole was called (Exod 4:22: bĕnî bĕkhōrî yisrāʾêl). ʿelyôn lĕmalkê-ʾāreṣ — the highest of all the kings of the earth. ʿElyôn (the highest) — the same word used of God as Most High now applied to the Davidic king's status among earthly kings.
28: lĕʿôlām ʾeshmar-lô ḥasdî — I will maintain my steadfast love for him forever. Shāmar ḥesed (to keep/maintain steadfast love — the covenant-keeping idiom) with lĕʿôlām (forever) — the duration of the ḥesed matches the duration of the throne (v4: forever). ûberîtî neʾĕmenet lô — and my covenant with him will be secure. Neʾĕmenet (niphal participle of ʾāman: reliable, faithful, established) — the covenant is structurally sound, not subject to failure from God's side.
29: wĕśamtî lāʿad zarʿô — his line will continue forever. Lāʿad (for ever, for the long duration) — the seed/line (zāraʿ) enduring through time. wĕkhissĕʾô kîmê shāmāyim — and his throne will last as long as the heavens above. Kîmê shāmāyim (like the days of the heavens) — the heavens as the measuring rod of the throne's duration. What endures as long as the physical universe endures does not end within history.
30: ʾim-yaʿăzĕbû bānāyw tôrātî — if his descendants abandon my instruction. The conditional clauses (vv30-32) are the covenant's discipline provisions — comparable to the blessings and curses of Deuteronomy. The ḥesed is unconditional (v28); the temporal experience of covenant blessing is conditional on obedience. ûbĕmishpāṭay lōʾ yēlēkhû — and refuse to live by my rulings. The two covenant-obligations: Torah and mišpāṭ (rulings, decisions — the case-law applications of Torah).
31: ʾim-ḥuqqōtay yĕḥallēlû — if they break my decrees. Ḥillēl (piel of ḥālal: to profane, to treat as common what is sacred — the same word as the profaning of the temple in Ps 74:7). wĕmiṣwōtay lōʾ yishmōrû — and fail to keep my commandments. The four terms across vv30-31 (tôrāh, mišpāṭ, ḥuqqôt, miṣwōt) cover the full vocabulary of Mosaic covenant stipulations — the discipline clauses are Deuteronomically comprehensive.
32: wĕphāqadtî bĕshebeṭ pishʿām — then I will punish their rebellion with a rod. Pāqad (to visit, to reckon with — the divine accounting verb used of both blessing and judgment). Shebeṭ (rod — the shepherd's staff used for discipline, cf. Ps 2:9; 2 Sam 7:14: God will be a father to David and discipline with a human rod). ûbingāʿîm ʿăwōnām — and their sin with blows. Negaʿ (blow, stroke, plague — the word for divine affliction that runs from Exod 11:1 through Ps 38:11) — the discipline is painful but not fatal to the covenant.
33: wĕḥasdî lōʾ-ʾāphîr mĕʿimmô — but I will not withdraw my steadfast love from him. ʾĀphar (hiphil: to break off, dissolve, annul) — the ḥesed cannot be dissolved. wĕlōʾ-ʾashqer bĕʾĕmûnātî — or betray my faithfulness. Shāqar (to deal falsely, to betray a trust) — negated: YHWH will not be false to his faithfulness. The divine attributes cannot be separated from divine action; what God is structurally, he does historically.
34: lōʾ-ʾăḥallēl bĕrîtî — I will not break my covenant. Ḥillēl (piel of ḥālal: to profane, to treat as common) — the same verb as the Davidic descendants' possible profaning of the statutes (v31). God will not do to his own covenant what the rebellious descendants might do to his statutes. ûmôṣāʾ śĕphātay lōʾ ʾăshanneh — or change what I have spoken. Môṣāʾ śĕphātayim (what goes out from the lips) — the spoken word once uttered is binding; the divine speech cannot be recalled or revised.
35: ʾaḥat nishbaʿtî bĕqodshî — I have sworn by my own holiness once and for all. ʾAḥat (once — the single, unrepeatable oath) + bĕqodshî (by my holiness) — God swears by his own holiness because there is nothing higher by which to swear (Heb 6:13). The holiness of God is the ultimate guarantee: if the oath fails, the divine holiness itself would be compromised. ʾim-lĕdāwid ʾăkhazzēb — I will not deceive David. Kāzab (to lie, deceive) negated — the oath sworn on divine holiness cannot deceive.
36: zarʿô lĕʿôlām yihyeh — his descendants will endure forever. The seed (zāraʿ) given permanence parallel to the throne. wĕkhissĕʾô kashshemesh negdî — his throne will last as long as the sun shines before me. Negdî (before me — in my sight, under my watching eye). The sun as the standard of permanence: the throne endures as long as the sun in YHWH's sight.
37: kĕyārēaḥ yikkôn lĕʿôlām — like the moon it will be established forever. The moon as the second celestial witness to permanence — the two great lights of Gen 1:16 both testify to the covenant's duration. wĕʿēd bashshaḥaq neʾĕmān selāh — that faithful witness set in the heavens. ʿĒd neʾĕmān (faithful witness) — the moon itself as a reliable witness to the covenant. The selah marks the theological climax of the covenant celebration — immediately followed by the devastating reversal of v38.
38: wĕʾattāh zānachtā wattimʾās — but now you have cast off and rejected him. The abrupt turn: wĕʾattāh (but you — the same emphatic pronoun as Ps 73:23's pivot, now in reverse direction). Zānaḥ (to cast off — Ps 74:1; 79:5's lament word) + māʾas (to reject) — the two rejection verbs together. hitʾabbartā ʿim-mĕshîḥekā — you are enraged against your anointed king. Mĕshîaḥ (the anointed one — the specific Davidic title; the Messiah). The psalm's entire covenant celebration becomes the indictment: the God who swore that oath on his holiness appears to have broken it.
39: niʾartā berît ʿabdekā — you have repudiated the covenant with your servant. Nāʾar (piel: to spurn, reject with contempt — to shake off as one shakes off something disgusting) — applied to the covenant. ḥillaltā lāʾāreṣ nizrô — you have thrown his crown into the dust. Ḥillēl (piel: to profane) — the crown (nāzar: the diadem, the consecrated crown) thrown to the ground. The divine holiness-oath of v35 is implicitly invoked: if God profanes the covenant and the crown, his own holiness is at stake.
40: pāraṣtā kol-gĕdērōtāyw — you have broken down all his walls. Pāraṣ (to break through, make a breach) — the defensive walls of the kingdom breached. śamtā miḇṣārāyw meḥittāh — and reduced his fortifications to rubble. Miḇṣār (fortification, stronghold) — the military infrastructure of the kingdom made into meḥittāh (terror, ruin — what causes fright).
41: shāsahû kol-ʿōbĕrê dārekh — all who pass by strip him of what he has. Shāsāh (to plunder, strip bare) — the king whose throne was sworn to last as long as the heavens (v29) is now stripped by passersby. hāyāh ḥerpāh lishkhēnāyw — he has become a mockery to his neighbors. Ḥerpāh (reproach, mockery — the shame vocabulary of Ps 79:4 at the destruction of Jerusalem). The king who should have been the highest of earthly kings (v27) is a mockery to neighbors.
42: hărîmōtā yĕmîn ṣārāyw — you have strengthened the hand of his enemies. The right hand of the enemies raised — the same right hand that God stretches out in salvation (v13) is here raised in the enemy's favor. hiśśaḥtā kol-śōnĕʾāyw — you have let all who hate him celebrate. Śāmaḥ (to rejoice) in the enemies — the joy that should belong to Israel (vv16) belongs to those who hate.
43: ʾap-tāshîb ṣûr ḥarbô — you have blunted his sword in battle. Shûb ṣûr ḥereb (to turn back the edge of the sword — to make it dull, to reverse its cutting power). wĕlōʾ hăqēmōtô bammilḥāmāh — and failed to hold him up when he fought. Qûm (hiphil: to establish, support) — God who upheld the Davidic king (v21: hand steadily upholding) has not upheld him in battle.
44: hishbattā miṭṭohŏrô — you have stripped away his glory. Ṭohar (purity, brightness, splendor) — the king's royal radiance removed. wĕkhissĕʾô lāʾāreṣ miggaretāh — and hurled his throne down to the ground. The throne sworn to stand as long as the heavens (v29) hurled to the ground — the contrast with the covenant's permanence is complete and devastating.
45: hiqṣartā yĕmê ʿălûmāyw — you have shortened the days of his prime. ʿĂlûmîm (youth, prime years — from ʿālam: young manhood, the vigorous years) — the king's best years cut short. heʿaṭîtāhû bûshāh selāh — and wrapped him in shame. Heʿāṭāh (to wrap, to clothe) — shame (bôshāh) worn as a garment (cf. Ps 73:6's pride-as-necklace). The selah marks the nadir of the catalog of disasters.
46: ʿad-māh YHWH tistātēr lāneṣaḥ — how long, O LORD? Will you hide yourself away forever? The ʿad-māh (how long?) of communal lament (Ps 74:10; 79:5) applied to the Davidic crisis. tiʿar kāʾesh ḥămātekā — how long will your anger blaze like fire? The divine anger as fire — the same image as Ps 79:5's jealousy burning. The question is not why God is angry but how long the anger can continue given the oath of v35.
47: zĕkōr-ʾănî māh-ḥāled — remember how short my span of life is! Ḥeled (span of life, lifetime — the brief duration of human existence; cf. Ps 39:5-6). The human-transience argument: God must act now because the Psalmist's life is short. ʿal-māh shāwĕʾ bārāʾtā kol-bĕnê-ʾādām — for what emptiness you have made all the children of man! Shāwĕʾ (emptiness, vanity — the Qoheleth word hebel in a different form) — if the covenant fails, human existence under it is vanity.
48: mî geber yiḥyeh wĕlōʾ yirʾeh māwet — what man can go on living and never see death? The universal human condition invoked as pressure: every human dies, so the anointed king under the covenant needs divine action before his death concludes the opportunity. yĕmalleṭ nafshô miyyad-shĕʾôl selāh — who can rescue his life from the grip of the grave? Shĕʾôl (the underworld) — from whose hand no human rescue is possible (Ps 49:7-9). The selah marks the unanswerable weight of mortality.
49: ʾayyēh ḥăsādêkhā hāriʾshōnîm ʾădōnāy — Lord, where is your steadfast love of old? The key appeal of the lament section: ḥăsādîm (the plural of ḥesed — the accumulated acts of steadfast love from ancient times, hāriʾshōnîm: the former ones). The plural ḥăsādîm includes all the covenant actions from the Exodus through David. nishbaʿtā lĕdāwid baʾĕmûnatekā — which in your faithfulness you swore to David? The oath of v35 invoked directly — the ʾĕmûnāh that was sworn is the basis for the appeal.
50: zĕkōr ʾădōnāy ḥerpat ʿăbādêkhā — remember, Lord, the mocking your servants endure. Zākar (to remember — the covenant memory-appeal) directed toward the mocking (ḥerpāh) of the divine servants. śĕʾētî bĕḥêqî kol-rabbîm ʿammîm — how I bear in my heart the taunts of all those nations. ḥêq (bosom, lap — what one carries close to oneself) — the Psalmist carries the nations' taunts in his own heart as a personal burden.
51: ʾăsher ḥērĕphû ʾôyĕbêkhā YHWH — with which your enemies, LORD, have taunted. The enemies of the Davidic king identified as enemies of YHWH — the attack on the anointed is an attack on the one who anointed him. ʾăsher ḥērĕphû ʿîqĕbôt mĕshîḥekā — mocking every step of your anointed king. ʿîqĕbôt (steps, footprints — every step the anointed takes is mocked). The psalm ends with the anointed king walking under constant derision — the lament is complete, without resolution, holding the tension between the oath of v35 and the reality of vv38-51.
52: bārûkh YHWH lĕʿôlām ʾāmēn wĕʾāmēn — blessed be the LORD forever! Amen and Amen. The doxology closing Book III of the Psalter (Pss 73-89) — identical in function to the doxologies of Books I (Ps 41:13), II (Ps 72:18-19), IV (Ps 106:48), and V (Ps 150). The double ʾāmēn (so be it — the congregational affirmation of what has been prayed) closes not the lament of Ps 89 but the entire third book. The lament of Ps 89 is not resolved within the psalm; the Book III doxology does not resolve it either — it holds the tension open for the entire Psalter to address in Books IV-V.
